title: Not Here
logline: You finish dead musicians' albums for a living. An anonymous estate hires you to a tidal-island studio to complete the final record of Wren Halloway, the vanished songwriter of the band Lightship — a band that slowly starts treating you as her replacement, and a client who knows the one secret you've never told anyone.
setting: The Foghorn: a residential recording studio built into a decommissioned foghorn station on Corrag Ness, a tidal island off Scotland's North Sea coast, October, off-season. The causeway drowns twice a day, so the tide is the game's clock — scenes are scheduled against it, and a three-day storm lock-in anchors Act II. Salt-damp piano, harmonium bolted to the wall, a 16-track tape machine, and 'SoundLedger,' the studio's ancient text-only session-logging computer. That last detail is the whole presentation strategy: the game IS the studio terminal. Both builds render typewritten session logs, ASCII mixer views, pinned ferry timetables, and tape-box labels from one shared engine core; the browser build layers in adaptive audio stems, tape grain, and dim colour, while the terminal build is the same fiction played on the machine the fiction is about. A text game set inside a logging terminal doesn't tolerate the terminal — it's canon there.
protagonist: The player is 'the Finisher' — a freelance engineer with a strange, half-famous specialty: completing the unfinished albums of dead and vanished artists. You are hired by 'the Halloway estate' through a lawyer's letter and paid in postal money orders. Your contract has one odd clause: nightly session logs must be uploaded to the estate. Voice is second-person present tense, written as those session logs — clipped, technical, observant, with grief leaking in around the edges of the jargon. One fact is fixed about you before the player customizes anything: you never released music of your own. You recorded exactly one demo, at seventeen, and mailed it once, to a PO box, and never spoke of it again. Character creation happens diegetically — Moira walks you through tuning the console and asks intake questions, ending with: 'Describe the first song you ever loved.' Your answer seeds your stats and, crucially, generates your personal musical motif. You will be hearing that motif for the rest of the game without knowing it.
statsDesign: Four visible stats, one hidden. EAR — what you can hear under things: gates finding buried vocal takes on old reels, noticing that a character's leitmotif stem detunes when they lie (the score is literally your lie detector), and early access to twist clues like matching handwriting. TACT — how you sit with other people's grief: gates whether confession scenes open or slam shut, softens confrontation outcomes. NERVE — willingness to push: gates playing the Aberdeen Tape at dinner, opening Moira's locked office, crossing the causeway at half-tide. CRAFT — actual engineering skill: gates restoring water-damaged tape, determines the finished album's quality tier, which several endings key off. And UNDERTOW — hidden until the reveal: how much of Wren you let them pour into you. It rises when you accept comparisons, wear her oilskin coat, sing her guide vocals instead of hiring them out, sleep in her room when yours floods. The game never shows you Undertow. It shows you the band getting happier. Intake seeds the four visible stats from your answers; every stat is checked in dialogue and work scenes, and no stat is safe to dump — a max-Craft, low-Tact run finishes a beautiful album for a band that never tells you the truth.

cast.name: Wren Halloway (the absent center)
cast.role: Lightship's songwriter and voice, vanished the night of October 3rd last year, presumed drowned off the ferry. Every room in the Foghorn is arranged around where she used to stand. She is also — though the player doesn't know it — 'the estate': your employer, alive in the mainland town of Ullscarf, reading your nightly session logs.
cast.want: To be free of the song 'Not Here' — the hit that built her cage — by having it finished and given back to the person it was taken from.
cast.secret: She didn't vanish; she walked. And the melody of 'Not Here' isn't hers. It came from a teenager's demo tape mailed to Lightship's PO box years ago — yours. She has hired the person she stole from.
cast.reactsToMemory: Because your contract uploads session logs nightly, Wren has diegetically read your entire playthrough. If you reach her in the Low Tide branch, she quotes your own logs back at you — including the exact wording of your intake answer about the first song you loved — and her warmth or wariness is computed from your whole run: how you treated Ciaran, whether you exposed the vote, how high your Undertow climbed. A player who 'became' Wren on the island meets a woman who flinches from them.

cast.name: Moira Skene
cast.role: Owner-producer of the Foghorn, mid-sixties, ran the desk for every Lightship record. Your point of contact, landlady, and the person who signs for the estate's money orders. Functions as mentor and gatekeeper of the physical space.
cast.want: To keep the Foghorn — and the version of events that lets everyone keep living — from going under.
cast.secret: Wren said goodbye to her. Moira has the leaving letter locked in her office drawer, has known for a year that Wren is alive, and forwards nothing to the police because Wren asked her not to. She also suspects who the estate really is and took the job anyway.
cast.reactsToMemory: The office drawer is openable in Act I with Nerve. If you read the letter and say nothing, Moira notices the letters have been moved — she never mentions it, but from then on she never leaves you alone in the office, and in Act III she burns the letter rather than let it surface. Only a player who never touched the drawer, or who confessed touching it, gets the scene where she hands the letter over herself.

cast.name: Dominic 'Dom' Ferro
cast.role: Bassist and de facto manager. The pragmatist keeping four people fed on one dead catalogue. He hired you over the others' objections and treats the album as a lifeboat with a label deadline stencilled on it.
cast.want: The record delivered by November 1st, because the label's advance is spent and he personally guaranteed the shortfall.
cast.secret: He called the green-room vote in Aberdeen that overrode Wren's plea to cancel the tour — and afterwards, he forged her signature on the contract extension that made the final leg legally binding. If she surfaces alive, the forgery surfaces with her.
cast.reactsToMemory: Dom tracks whether you side with him on schedule fights, scene by scene. If you humiliate him by playing the Aberdeen Tape at dinner, he doesn't rage — he quietly sabotages the title track's bass stem two days later, and only a high-Ear player catches the sour tuning before print. If you consistently backed him, he confesses the forgery unprompted in the storm, the only route to his real face.

cast.name: Etta Okonkwo
cast.role: Drummer. The band's warmth — cooks for everyone, names the studio mice, keeps morning coffee going. Will not enter the live room after dark and laughs it off every time.
cast.want: For the album to close the wound — to play these songs once, properly, and be allowed to stop grieving.
cast.secret: She was the last to see Wren. She didn't just see her — she drove her to the mainland slip at 2 a.m., gave her the cash in her gig bag, and told police it was the evening ferry. She smashed her own ride cymbal the night of the Aberdeen vote and has played with a hole in her kit ever since.
cast.reactsToMemory: The game silently counts whether you show up to her 7 a.m. kitchen coffee. Enough mornings and her Act III confession comes unprompted, over the sink, no stat check — the single warmest scene in the game. Skip the mornings and the same information requires a hard Tact gate in a much colder scene, and she asks you to leave the kitchen after.

cast.name: Ciaran Halloway
cast.role: Wren's younger brother, fiddle player, the one who never accepted 'presumed drowned.' His room is papered with current charts and ferry manifests. The band's open wound, and the player's mirror: he also lives inside a Wren-shaped hole.
cast.want: Proof. In either direction. He says he wants her found; what he actually wants is to be forgiven for his vote.
cast.secret: He voted with Dom in Aberdeen — her own brother, third hand up. And months ago he found Wren's oilskin coat cached above the tideline with a note in the pocket, and hid both, because the note's last line was addressed to him.
cast.reactsToMemory: Ciaran keeps a private tally of every time you accept a comparison to Wren — every 'she used to say that too' you let stand. High Undertow makes him the only character who turns on you: 'Stop wearing her.' Low Undertow plus sharing what you learn makes him your co-conspirator, and he's the required companion for the Low Tide crossing — he physically knows where the causeway holds at half-tide.

mystery: As the player experiences it, three questions braid together and tighten. First, the procedural one: what actually happened on October 3rd? The band's story — she took the last ferry and never arrived — starts shedding parts almost immediately; the kitchen timetable shows the evening ferry stopped running September 30th, and nobody will look at it. Second, the professional one: who is 'the Halloway estate'? Wren died intestate with a living brother in the next bedroom — so who is paying you, in money orders from a mainland town called Ullscarf, and why does the contract demand your session logs every night? Third, the one under your skin, which the game never states as a question: why does this music feel like yours? Why did the desk 'hum in your key' on day one? Why is the title track the only song on the liner mockup with a blank where the music credit should be? The band thinks the mystery is where Wren went. The player slowly realizes the mystery is why YOU, specifically, are here.
twist: The full reveal comes in two waves. Wave one — who actually left: Wren didn't vanish; she was left first. Fourteen months ago, mid-tour in Aberdeen, she broke down and begged the band to cancel the final leg. Dom called a vote in the green room: three hands to one, keep going — and the third hand was Ciaran's. A tour recorder left running on the board captured the whole thing. She finished the tour like a ghost, came home to Corrag Ness, and on October 3rd — a night with no ferry — Etta drove her to the mainland slip and lied to the police. 'She left us' is the story; the truth is they left her, in a green room, months before she ever packed a bag. The vanishing was her answer, and Moira's silence was her parting gift to her. Wave two — the player's true relationship: the estate is Wren, alive in Ullscarf, and she chose you by name. Because years ago, a seventeen-year-old finisher-to-be mailed one demo tape to Lightship's PO box. Wren kept it. The melody of 'Not Here' — the band's defining song, the unfinished title track, the main theme the player has heard since the title screen — is built on YOUR motif, the one 'generated' at character creation, which has been hiding inverted inside the score the entire game. She stole the song that made her famous from the person she has now hired to finish it. She cannot complete 'Not Here' because it was never hers to complete. The title detonates three ways: Wren is not here; the band's love has been aimed at someone not here; and the song's missing bridge — the thing no one could write for a year — is the answering voice of the one person who was never supposed to be here at all. You.
cluesInRetrospect: 1) The title-screen theme itself: the main theme of 'Not Here' contains the player's intake motif, inverted and slowed, from the first moment of the game — planted before the player even makes it. Moira even jokes on day one, 'Odd — the desk hums in your key.' After the reveal, returning to the title screen is the chills moment. 2) The liner mockup: every track reads 'words & music W. Halloway' except the title track — 'words W. Halloway / music —' and a blank. Dom waves it off as a typo. 3) The ferry timetable pinned in the kitchen: evening service ends September 30th. The band's unanimous 'last ferry on October 3rd' story has been impossible from Act I, in plain text, in both builds. 4) The hiring letter: a high-Ear player can notice in Act I that the 'lawyer's' handwriting shares a looping G with the marginalia in Wren's lyric notebooks; characters dismiss it. 5) The contract's nightly-log clause and the money orders postmarked Ullscarf — the same town as the unsigned postcard in Moira's office containing nothing but a pressed sprig of gorse ('gorse still burns' is a line from an unreleased Wren lyric the player restores in Act II). Someone is reading everything you do, and paying from where the postcard came from. 6) Etta's kit is missing its ride cymbal — 'cracked in Aberdeen,' she shrugs — and her leitmotif has an audible hole where the crash should land. The score has been telling you where the wound happened since her first scene.
actStructure: ACT 0 — INTAKE (30 min): Arrival at low tide. Diegetic character creation: tuning the console seeds Ear/Tact/Nerve/Craft; the 'first song you loved' free-text moment generates your motif. ACT I — SETUP DAYS (the song economy begins): Eight unfinished songs sit on the shelf; the November 1st deadline means you can fully finish only about four. Each song is bound to one character's grief — choosing which reels to restore IS choosing whose confession path opens. Soft branching: relationships, clue access, morning-coffee-style habit trackers. MIDPOINT — THE ABERDEEN TAPE (first hard fork): Restoring a tour board-mix, you find the green-room vote, recorded. Four irrevocable options: play it at dinner for everyone (Nerve-gated) → CONFRONTATION FORK, Act II becomes fracture-and-confession, sessions turn into reckonings; play it privately for one person → LEVERAGE FORK, you choose whose Act II transforms, everyone else's stays sealed; say nothing and keep working → CONCEALMENT FORK, the album advances fastest while the rot spreads under it; destroy it → ASH FORK, Ciaran finds the residue in the burn barrel and Act II turns him against you. ACT II — THE STORM: A three-day lock-in, tide and weather sealing the island; each fork plays radically different storm scenes; Undertow accumulates through dozens of small accept-or-deflect choices; the toast scene (second LLM touchpoint) lands here. ACT III — THE CROSSING (second hard fork): The Ullscarf thread completes (letter, postmark, handwriting). With the deadline days away you must choose: STAY and finish 'Not Here' — which can only be completed by knowingly contributing your motif, then deciding whether to sign it, gift it anonymously, or refuse — or GO: cross at low tide with (or without) Ciaran to find Wren, abandoning the album to the deadline. Endings resolve from fork × truth-state × Undertow × album quality × the credit decision.

endings.name: The Album (Signed)
endings.howReached: Stay for the deadline after the truth is at least partly out (Confrontation or Leverage fork), finish 'Not Here,' and take the co-writing credit under your own name. Requires mid-or-better Craft.
endings.tone: Bittersweet triumph — the band survives as something smaller and honest; the record ships with two names on the title track; Wren's only response is a single unsigned line forwarded by the 'estate': 'It finally lands.'

endings.name: The Empty Chair
endings.howReached: Concealment fork carried all the way: high Craft, truth never surfaces, album finished under the lie and released to acclaim.
endings.tone: Cold and haunting — the launch playback scene, the finished song filling a room of people who cannot look at each other, the band quietly dissolving as the last chorus plays. Commercial success as a wake.

endings.name: Low Tide
endings.howReached: Choose the crossing: assemble the Ullscarf clue chain, keep Ciaran as an ally (low Undertow), abandon the album, and cross the causeway to find Wren alive.
endings.tone: Quiet and healing — no confrontation scene, no album; you and Wren play the seventeen-year-old demo version of 'Not Here' once, in her kitchen, for no one. The deadline passes offscreen.

endings.name: Undertow
endings.howReached: Hidden-stat ending: consistently accept the projection — her coat, her room, her guide vocals, her comparisons — until Undertow crosses its threshold, regardless of fork.
endings.tone: Horror in soft focus — the band is happy, the album is finished, the press photo has five people in it, and the final session log is signed with a W. The title recontextualizes one last time: now YOU are not here.

endings.name: The Bonfire
endings.howReached: Ash fork: destroy the Aberdeen Tape, get caught by Ciaran, and let the storm scenes escalate to open rupture; the album collapses.
endings.tone: Raw and cathartic — truths shouted over weather, the band ends on the beach, but the last scene is you and Ciaran on the mainland road with a box of current charts, going to look for her the honest way. An ending that is also a beginning.

endings.name: Anonymous
endings.howReached: The near-golden path: finish the confession songs of all four residents, surface the truth with Tact rather than force, complete 'Not Here,' and gift your motif anonymously — refusing the credit while threading the truth into the liner notes where only she will see it.
endings.tone: Grace note — post-credits, a new postcard arrives at the Foghorn addressed to you by name for the first time, and under the main theme, faintly, a second wren answers back.

endings.name: No Music
endings.howReached: Refuse both forks: don't finish the title track, don't cross. Take the ferry when service resumes. Always available; deliberately anticlimactic.
endings.tone: Numb, deliberately unresolved — the game lets you leave the way Wren did. But the engine remembers: next run, the console hums in your previous key, and Moira hesitates before saying hello.

musicIdentity: The score is the mystery. Main theme: 'Not Here' is a folk melody engineered to never resolve — it ends its every phrase on the second scale degree, hanging, and the full cadence does not exist anywhere in the game's audio until an ending in which the song is finished. The player's intake motif (chosen from twelve authored motifs, parameterized by the intake) is secretly the counter-melody that completes it; the title screen has contained the answer since minute one. Per-character leitmotifs are built from instrument + wound: MOIRA is harmonium and foghorn — long drone tones a minor third apart, the actual foghorn interval, patience with a warning inside it. DOM is an upright-bass ostinato that plays slightly ahead of the beat, always rushing the band, the deadline made audible. ETTA is brushed-kit heartbeat with a hole in it — her pattern builds to a ride-cymbal accent that never comes, because the cymbal cracked in Aberdeen; the first time the crash actually lands is her confession scene. CIARAN is fiddle quoting Wren's theme a fifth up and stopping two bars short, every time. WREN is the main theme entire. Recombination is systemic: dialogue scenes are adaptive stem mixes — trust raises a character's stem in the mix, and lying detunes it by a quarter-tone, which high-Ear players learn to hear, making the score a literal lie detector. Story beats are scored by collision: the Aberdeen Tape scene plays all four band motifs simultaneously fighting for the same bar; the 3 a.m. completion scene assembles the full arrangement one player-earned stem at a time until the player motif enters and the theme lands on tonic for the first time in the entire game. Palette: harmonium, fiddle, nylon guitar, upright bass, brushed kit, boy-soprano-like whistle for Wren's absent voice, tape hiss and foghorn as pitched instruments. Dual-platform: the score is authored as event data (melody/harmony/stems), rendered through Web Audio stem-mixing in the browser and through a chiptune/FM synth voice in the terminal (with optional bundled stem playback where a local audio player exists). Every motif is deliberately melody-first — written to survive on a 1-bit beeper — so the leitmotif system, including the lie-detune tell and the final cadence, is fully intact in a pure terminal.
llmTouchpoints: Exactly four, all narrow, all with deterministic fallbacks, none of which write the story. (1) INTAKE MOTIF — 'Describe the first song you ever loved and why.' The LLM maps free text to stat seeds, selects one of twelve fully authored motifs, and extracts two short player phrases that characters quote back later ('you called it the song that made the kitchen bigger'). Fallback: a five-question multiple-choice intake mapping to the same twelve motifs — the game is 100% playable offline. (2) THE TOAST — storm night, the player writes a free-text toast 'to the person this room misses.' The LLM classifies tone only (comfort / challenge / deflection / becoming-her) to route among authored responses and adjust Undertow, and saves one player phrase for an ending callback. Fallback: choose from six authored toasts with the same routing. (3) TWO LINES — on one mid-album song, the player may write two lyric lines. The LLM checks meter and content-safety and selects the closest of six authored melodic settings; the words displayed and sung are the player's verbatim — player-authored, never LLM-written. Fallback: line construction from a curated word bank. (4) WREN'S REPLY (optional, NG+ only) — if the player writes a letter to Wren after certain endings, the LLM generates her short reply under a hard style guide and ending-state constraints, clearly framed as an epilogue extra. Fallback: authored replies per ending, which are also the defaults. The branch structure, twist, clue placement, and every ending are authored and deterministic; the LLM only ever personalizes texture inside authored rails.
emotionalCore: The game is about being loved as a stand-in — being needed for the shape of you rather than the fact of you — and the almost unbearable relief of the moment someone finally sees you instead. Every character does it: the band pours Wren into the player-shaped hole; the player, if they're honest, came to this island because finishing other people's music is safer than being heard. Everything builds toward one scene: 3 a.m. in the live room after the storm, tape rolling, the band attempting the title track one last time — and when the player's motif enters as the bridge, the arrangement locks, the theme resolves to tonic for the first time in the whole game, and one by one they stop playing to listen, until Ciaran says, quietly, into the room: 'She didn't write that part. Did she.' Four people looking at you — actually at you — for the first time. The music has been grieving in the second degree for ten hours of play, and it finally comes home in the same instant the player does.
replayHook: The song economy is the primary pull: eight songs, time for four — half the confession paths, clues, and character truths are structurally unreachable in one run, and the two hard forks (Aberdeen Tape × the Crossing) multiply against them. Twelve intake motifs mean your theme — and therefore the literal sound of the main theme's hidden counter-melody — differs between runs. The hidden Undertow ending is nearly impossible to hit blind and becomes a deliberate, uncomfortable project on replay. NG+ is diegetic memory: the Foghorn's console 'remembers' — it hums in your previous run's key on boot, Moira half-recognizes you ('we had a finisher before… you remind me of them'), a thirteenth motif unlocks, and a tape reel labeled with your previous run's real completion date sits on the shelf containing your previous run's actual final mix, playable in-game. Cross-run threads: the Anonymous ending's postcard arrives in Act I of your next run; the No Music ending seeds Moira's hesitation at the door. The save file is framed as the studio's tape archive, so remembering across runs isn't a feature — it's the premise.
whyNotUndertaleClone: It shares Undertale's principles and none of its body. No combat, no bullet-hearts, no mercy system, no monster town: this is a studio drama played as a procedural of grief. The convention it subverts is different, too — Undertale interrogates RPG violence; Not Here interrogates the 'you are the chosen one' frame itself: you are explicitly the replacement, hired help arriving into someone else's story, and the game is about the ethics of letting people project onto you. The leitmotif principle is honored but mechanically novel rather than borrowed: motifs here are evidence (the twist is hidden in the score), instrumentation (lie-detune as a listening mechanic), and authorship (the final act turns on who gets credit for a melody) — music as plot, not just as memory-trigger. Consequence-memory is expressed through stem mixes, habit trackers, and a diegetic tape archive rather than a genocide-route meta. And tonally it refuses Undertale's comedy-to-tragedy whiplash for something closer to To The Moon by way of a working recording session: the warmth comes from morning coffee and take counts, not jokes — a grown-up grief piece that happens to be a mystery you can hear.
